unitTitle: Unit 19: Questions / Question tags / Indirect questions

rows: With *be* as a main verb

**Am**/**Was** I early?
**Are**/**Were** you/we/they ready?
**Is**/**Was** he/she/it lost?
With *be* as an auxiliary verb

**Am**/**Was** I **helping** you enough?
**Are**/**Were** you/we/they **travelling** by train?
**Is**/**Was** he/she/it **staying** at the hotel?
With *have* as an auxiliary verb

**Have**/**Had** I/you/we/they finished the task?
**Has**/**Had** he/she/it sent the email?
With *have* as a main verb and with all other verbs

**Do**/**Did** I/you/we/they have enough time?
**Does**/**Did** he/she/it need the information?
With modals

**Should** I turn left here?
**Could** you lend me a pen?
**Will** he be there on time?
**Might** they be stuck in traffic?
With *who, whose, whom, what, which, where, when, why* and *how*

**Who** is giving the presentation?
**Whose** laptop is this?
To **whom** should I address this letter?
**What** is today's special?
**Which** would you prefer?
**Where** did you spend last Christmas?
**When** is Paul starting his new job?
**Why** did they move offices?
**How** do you say 'receipt' in French?

items.note: With the question words *who* and *what*, whether we use *do* or not depends on whether the question word refers to the **subject** or **object** of the verb.

items.correct: Subject: *Who **called** you?* (= Someone called you. Who?)
Object: *Who **did** you **call**?* (= You called someone. Who?)

items.note: Remember that after *do* or *does*, we use the bare infinitive.

items.correct: *Did you **go** to the talk on renewable energy?*

items.incorrect: *Did you **went** to the talk on renewable energy?*

items.note: Remember that the verb *mean* forms questions just like other main verbs.

rows: With *be* as a main verb

You **are** from New Zealand, **aren't** you?
She **is** a good teacher, **isn't** she?
With auxiliary verbs and modals

You **haven't** taken my jacket, **have** you?
We **are** having a meeting early tomorrow, **aren't** we?
People **should** turn off lights when they leave, **shouldn't** they?
There **will** be plenty of seats, **won't** there?
With *have* as a main verb
Marco **has** a wonderful singing voice, **hasn't**/**doesn't** he?
With other verbs

You play chess, **don't** you?
Margaret works in the city centre now, **doesn't** she?
Your friends really enjoyed the concert last night, **didn't** they?
With *Let's*
Let's order a pizza tonight, **shall** we?
With imperatives

Hand me that report, **will**/**would**/**could** you?
Don't forget the meeting tomorrow, **will** you?

content.subtitle: Use
items: To ask someone to agree with us (falling intonation): *It's really cold today, **isn't** it?*
To check whether something is true (rising intonation): *You're from Argentina, **aren't** you?*

items.note: In sentences with *I am*, we use *aren't I?* as the question tag. In sentences with *I am not*, we use *am I?*

items.correct: I'm the tallest person in the team, **aren't** I?

items.note: With *everyone*, *no one* and *someone*, we use question tags with a plural verb and *they*.

items.correct: Everyone's going to love this, **aren't** they?
No one wants to stay late, **do** they?
Someone's been using my computer, **haven't** they?

items.note: Usually, when we have a positive verb in a sentence, we use a negative question tag. When we have a negative verb in a sentence, we use a positive question tag. In sentences with a negative word like *no, little, never, nobody, no one, hardly*, etc, we use a positive question tag.
In sentences where the subject is *there*, we repeat *there* in the question tag.

items.correct: There's no point contacting Kim now, **is there**?

content.subtitle: American English note
content.description: In American English, a question tag with *do* can be used after a sentence with *have got*. This is not usually done in British English.
examples: US: They've got a huge apartment, **don't** they? UK: They've got a huge apartment, **haven't** they?

content.subtitle: Form
content.formula: introductory phrase or question + clause with normal word order

content.subtitle: Use
content.description: We use indirect questions when we want to ask questions politely.

rows: *Can/Could you tell me ...?*
*Could you tell me what time the shop closes?*
*Could you let me know ...?*
*Could you let me know when the results are published?*
*Do you know ...?*
*Do you know who the new director is?*
*I wonder if you could tell me ...*
*I wonder if you could tell me how much this item costs.*
*I wonder if you know ...*
*I wonder if you know what the application deadline is.*
*I would like to know ...*
*I would like to know what your organisation is planning to do about it.*

items.note: We **do not** use question word order in the second part of the sentence.

items.correct: I would like to know when **the last bus to the city centre leaves**.

items.incorrect: I would like to know when **does the last bus to the city centre leave**.

items.note: Some indirect questions, which begin with a question word, need a question mark at the end.

items.correct: ***Could** you tell me where the ticket office is?*

items.note: If a direct question is a 'yes/no' question, the equivalent indirect question uses *if* or *whether*.

items.correct: I wonder ***if***/**whether** you have stayed here before.

items.sentence: Sorry to interrupt! ...... keeping you from something?
Did the manager ...... you about the schedule change?
...... warm when you visited Portugal?
Maria, ...... got time to email Mr García?
When the client arrived, ...... finished the presentation?
I know he's talented, but ...... seen anyone practise so little before a big performance?
It's already midnight, so ...... still be open?
...... help me carry these boxes upstairs, please?
Professor, ...... go over the assignment instructions once more, please?
...... the guests you invited to the conference last month coming to the ceremony?
I know how to spell 'necessary', but what ......?
It's a bold plan, but ...... it will actually succeed?

sections.title: B: Write questions
sections.instructions: Write questions using the words given.

items.sentence: you / chat / online / when I called?
items.answer: Were you chatting online when I called?

items.sentence: Sam / send / you / her phone number / this morning?
items.answer: Did Sam send you her phone number this morning?

items.sentence: you / always / have / breakfast / this early?
items.answer: Do you always have breakfast this early?

items.sentence: Lucy and Dan / come / to the barbecue / on Saturday?
items.answer: Are Lucy and Dan coming to the barbecue on Saturday?

items.sentence: you / can / give / me / a lift / later?
items.answer: Can you give me a lift later?

items.sentence: how / you / say / that word / in Spanish?
items.answer: How do you say that word in Spanish?

items.sentence: why / the council / can't / do / something / about the traffic?
items.answer: Why can't the council do something about the traffic?

items.sentence: where / you / go / on / your trip / last summer?
items.answer: Where did you go on your trip last summer?

items.sentence: what / the new office / look like / when / it is finished?
items.answer: What will the new office look like when it is finished?

items.sentence: which / type of coffee / your favourite / be?
items.answer: Which type of coffee is your favourite?

sections.title: C: Question words
sections.instructions: Write a question word in each gap.

items.sentence: ...... bag is this? Is it yours?
...... do you usually get up?
...... is the man in the blue shirt?
...... didn't you call me last night?
...... do you switch this device on?
...... did you order at the restaurant?
...... route do you prefer? The coastal road or the motorway?
...... is the nearest bank from here?

sections.title: D: Subject vs. object questions
sections.instructions: Rewrite the sentences to make them into questions. Begin with the word in brackets.

items.sentence:
I asked the teacher.
**who** ......

I met my old classmate.
**who** ......

My sister bought me a book.
**who** ......

I thought you were in London.
**what** ......
items.answer: What made you think I was in London?

items.sentence:
Mr Harris taught us maths.
**who** ......

I borrowed a bike from Alex.
**who** ......
items.answer: Who did you borrow a bike from?

items.sentence:
My neighbour brought me some soup when I was ill.
**who** ......

Harry blamed me for losing his keys.
**who** ......
items.answer: Who did Harry blame for losing his keys?

items.sentence:
I look up to my grandfather more than anyone else.
**who** ......
items.answer: Who do you look up to more than anyone else?

items.sentence:
I told Emma the good news.
**who** ......
items.answer: Who told Emma the good news?

sections.title: E: Question words (gap fill)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: (1) ...... you know where the library is?
(2) ...... didn't you let me know you were going to be late?
What (3) ...... I need to bring to the interview?
What (4) ...... this icon mean on the screen?
(5) ...... you point me in the right direction, please?
(6) ...... did you buy that jacket? In Milan?
(7) ...... do you know my address?
(8) ...... are you staring at?
You've replied to that email I forwarded you, **D** ...haven't you?
You take the underground to work, **A** ...don't you?
You won't mention this to anyone, **C** ...will you?
You're a colleague of Alice's, **H** ...aren't you?
You were living in Tokyo at that time, **G** ...weren't you?
You never stay in the office past six, **E** ...do you?
You made no attempt to apologise to Ryan, **F** ...did you?
You got your sister a scarf for her birthday, **B** ...didn't you?
Grab me a bottle of water on your way back, ...... you?
Let's watch that documentary you recorded last night, ...... we?
There's not really much point arguing about it, ...... there?
Katie will give us a lift to the station, ...... she?
Nobody seems to like this new design, ...... they?
I'm not explaining this very well, ...... I?
Let's leave early because the traffic gets bad later, ...... it?
If you borrow my umbrella, don't lose it, ...... you?
Max should be back from lunch by now, ...... he?
I'm making you feel nervous, ...... I?
Somebody left the window open overnight, ...... they?
Nobody's heard about this yet, ...... they?
I wonder if you could tell me what time does the last train to Edinburgh leave?
items.answer: I wonder if you could tell me what time the last train to Edinburgh leaves.

items.sentence: Could you let me know when would you like me to arrive for the interview.
items.answer: Could you let me know when you would like me to arrive for the interview?

items.sentence: I wonder if you know which platform should I go to for the London service.
items.answer: I wonder if you know which platform I should go to for the London service.

items.sentence: Do you think you could tell me how do I use this self-checkout machine?
items.answer: Do you think you could tell me how I use this self-checkout machine?

items.sentence: I wonder you have met Professor Williams?
items.answer: I wonder if you have met Professor Williams./I wonder whether you have met Professor Williams.

items.sentence: I would like to know do you have any single rooms available?
items.answer: I would like to know if you have any single rooms available./I would like to know whether you have any single rooms available.

items.sentence: Can you tell me what were you doing in my office?
items.answer: Can you tell me what you were doing in my office?

items.sentence: Do you know where is the nearest pharmacy?
items.answer: Do you know where the nearest pharmacy is?

sections.title: I: Key word transformation (Indirect questions)
sections.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
When does Rachel get back from Paris?
**know** Do ...... back from Paris?

What time does the exhibition open tomorrow?
**opens** Could you tell me ...... tomorrow?
items.answer: what time the exhibition opens/when the exhibition opens

items.sentence:
Is lunch included in the price?
**know** I would like to ...... in the price.
items.answer: know if lunch is included/know whether lunch is included

items.sentence:
What is the starting salary for this role?
**let** Could you ...... the starting salary is?

Have you visited Madrid before?
**wonder** I ...... Madrid before.
items.answer: wonder if you have visited/wonder whether you have visited

items.sentence:
Did Tom pass his driving test?
**passed** Do you know ...... his driving test?

I wonder if you know where Sarah went after the team dinner last week.
**go** Where ...... after the team dinner last week?

I would like to know how many days of annual leave we receive each year.
**given** How many days of annual leave ...... each year?

sections.title: J: Error correction (extra words)
sections.instructions: There is one extra word in each line. Click the extra word.

items.sentence: Are you free at the weekend or are you be quite busy?
I would like to know it when I can expect my delivery to arrive.
Do you think whether you could possibly let me know how soon you will have the project finished?
I wonder if you know who it is in charge of organising the event.
Tell Lucy who did you meet when you were at the conference last week.
Did Max mention who he sold him the tickets to?
You shouldn't leave your application until the very last minute if you want to be considered, should not you?
I wonder it if you know where I can find a good place to eat nearby.
